name: Moncayo Summit Loop
name_original: Ascensión a la cumbre del Moncayo desde la Fuente de los Frailes
description: Ascend to Moncayo's summit from Fuente de los Frailes on this difficult 23.4-mile mountain bike route with panoramic views.
region_intro: Mountain biking in Moncayo offers diverse terrain across the Moncayo Natural Park, characterized by its dramatic landscapes and varied ecosystems. The region features steep slopes, rocky outcrops, and high peaks, including San Miguel Peak, the highest summit in the Sistema Ibérico range. Riders can explore extensive beech and pine forests at higher altitudes, transitioning to holm oak at lower elevations, providing a rich and changing backdrop for mountain bike trails. Best mountain bike trails in Moncayo The most popular mountain bike trail is Moncayo Loop, a 30.2 miles (48.7 km) difficult trail that takes 4 hours 36 minutes to complete, featuring significant elevation gain. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Moncayo Summit Loop, a difficult 23.4 miles (37.6 km) path that ascends over 1400 meters through varied forest types. Local mountain bikers also love the Cerro Gordo MTB Route, an 18.0 miles (29.0 km) trail leading through the natural park's diverse landscapes, often completed in about 2 hours 50 minutes. Mountain biking in Moncayo is defined by high peaks, varied forests, and challenging topography. The region's network of routes offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Moncayo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 200 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Moncayo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are available in Moncayo?
A: Moncayo offers a wide selection of mountain bike trails, with over 35 routes available for exploration. These trails cater to various skill levels, from moderate to very difficult.

Q: What is the overall difficulty level of mountain biking in Moncayo?
A: The mountain biking trails in Moncayo are predominantly moderate to difficult. There are 17 moderate trails and 19 difficult trails, with a few options suitable for beginners. The region's terrain, characterized by steep slopes and high peaks, often presents a significant challenge.

Q: Are there mountain bike trails suitable for beginners in Moncayo?
A: While Moncayo is known for its challenging terrain, there is at least one trail categorized as easy, making it suitable for beginners. Most routes, however, are moderate to difficult, so beginners should carefully check route details before setting out.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on Moncayo's mountain bike trails?
A: You can expect highly varied terrain in Moncayo. Trails traverse dramatic landscapes with steep slopes, rocky outcrops, and diverse forests. You'll ride through extensive beech and pine forests at higher altitudes, transitioning to holm oak at lower elevations. Some routes also feature glacial cirques and natural springs.

Q: What natural attractions can I see while mountain biking in Moncayo?
A: Moncayo's trails offer access to stunning natural features. You might encounter the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5288045" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cueva de los Pilares&lt;/a&gt;, a natural rock sanctuary, or enjoy panoramic views from viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5291398" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de la Mata&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3924334" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo Summit&lt;/a&gt; itself is a significant landmark, and you can also explore areas like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5271458" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valcongosto Gorge&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any cultural or historical landmarks accessible by mountain bike in Moncayo?
A: Yes, beyond the natural beauty, routes often pass by significant attractions. The Santuario de la Virgen del Moncayo is a historic mountain sanctuary. Additionally, you can explore charming towns like Calcena, Purujosa, Talamantes, and Tarazona, which offer rich cultural and historical heritage and serve as excellent starting points for rides.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes in Moncayo?
A: Yes, many of Moncayo's mountain bike routes are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e985076453/moncayo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 48.7 km ride, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e986801726/moncayo-summit-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo Summit Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is 37.6 km long.

Q: What do other mountain bikers say about the trails in Moncayo?
A: The mountain bike trails in Moncayo are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 10 reviews. Riders often praise the diverse terrain, the stunning natural beauty, and the challenging yet rewarding ascents through varied forest types.

Q: Are there any particularly challenging mountain bike routes for experienced riders?
A: Experienced riders seeking a challenge will find plenty in Moncayo. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e986809904/calcena-purujosa-trasobares-calcena-circular-por-la-cara-oculta-del-moncayo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calcena - Purujosa - Trasobares - Calcena circular&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain over 54 km. The region is also known for routes with steep slopes and rocky sections, providing a true test of skill and endurance.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the mountain bike trails in Moncayo?
A: Moncayo Natural Park is generally dog-friendly, but it's essential to keep your dog under control, especially due to the presence of wildlife. Always check specific trail regulations or park guidelines for any restrictions on dogs, particularly in protected areas or during certain seasons.

Q: Are there any family-friendly mountain bike routes in Moncayo?
A: While many routes in Moncayo are moderate to difficult, there are some easier sections or trails that could be suitable for families with older children or those with some mountain biking experience. It's best to look for routes specifically marked as 'easy' or those with less elevation gain and smoother terrain to ensure a pleasant family outing.

Q: What is the best time of year for mountain biking in Moncayo?
A: The best time for mountain biking in Moncayo is typically during spring and autumn when temperatures are milder and the natural scenery is vibrant. Summer can be hot, especially at lower elevations, while winter can bring snow and ice to higher altitudes, making some trails inaccessible or more challenging.

Q: Is parking available near the mountain bike trails in Moncayo?
A: Yes, parking is generally available in and around the towns bordering Moncayo Natural Park, such as Tarazona, Calcena, Purujosa, and Talamantes. These towns often serve as starting points for many routes, and you can usually find designated parking areas. Always check local signage for any specific parking regulations.
tips: We are in a picturesque spot on the GR 90.1, on the path from Ágreda to Ambel near the ravine of the Morca
At this point of the GR 90.1 you have magnificent views of the Moncayo peak.

Located at an altitude of 2,314.3 meters, we arrive at Pico Moncayo, within the Natural Park of the same name. The panoramic views are splendid, but the route to the peak is also splendid, passing through the beautiful oak forest. Do not forget your coat, in any season the wind blows in Moncayo!

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this mountain bike route?
faqs.answer: This route is considered difficult and requires very good physical condition and experience with mountain biking. You should be prepared for significant elevation gain and challenging terrain, including sections where you may need to push your bike.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the ascent to Moncayo from Fuente de los Frailes?
faqs.answer: The trail begins on a short paved section before transitioning to a well-marked path through a dense forest of pines, beeches, and oaks. As you gain elevation, the terrain becomes rockier with loose stones and rocks, especially on steep ramps. Near the summit, you'll encounter rocky steps and potentially icy conditions, particularly in winter, including the notorious 'escupidera' section.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required to ride this trail in Moncayo Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for mountain biking within the Moncayo Natural Park. However, it's always advisable to check local regulations before your visit, especially regarding protected areas and specific activities. Please respect the park's rules and natural environment.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to attempt this ascent?
faqs.answer: The best times to ride this trail are typically late spring, summer, and early autumn. During these seasons, the weather is generally more stable, and the risk of snow and ice at higher elevations is lower. Winter ascents can be very challenging and dangerous due to ice, snow, and strong winds, especially near the summit.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest or views along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse scenery. You'll ride through beautiful forests of pine, beech, and oak. As you climb higher, the forest gives way to reveal the impressive glacial cirque of San Miguel. From the Moncayo Summit, you'll be rewarded with extensive panoramic views, potentially stretching from the Cantabrian Mountains to the Pyrenees on clear days. You'll also get a great View of Moncayo itself.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for mountain bikers with dogs?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally allowed in Moncayo Natural Park, it's crucial to keep them on a leash, especially due to the presence of wildlife and other trail users. Given the difficult nature of this mountain biking route, with challenging terrain and potentially dangerous sections, consider if it's appropriate for your dog's fitness and safety.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this route?
faqs.answer: The route typically starts from Fuente de los Frailes. You should be able to find parking facilities in or near this area, which serves as a common trailhead for ascents to Moncayo. It's always a good idea to arrive early, especially on busy days, to secure a spot.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with or runs alongside sections of other trails. You may encounter parts of the Circular del Moncayo, Etapa 2: Santuario del Moncayo - Purujosa, and Barranco del Apio. It also briefly touches upon sections of the GR 90.1: Path from Ágreda to Ambel.

faqs.question: Are there any water sources available along the trail?
faqs.answer: While the trail itself might not have many direct water sources, the route passes near the Santuario del Moncayo, which is a known point where you can refill water. It's always recommended to carry sufficient water for the entire duration of this challenging ride, especially during warmer months.

faqs.question: What should I be aware of regarding weather conditions on Moncayo?
faqs.answer: Moncayo is known for its rapidly changing weather, especially at higher elevations. Strong winds are common and can significantly affect perceived temperatures. Be prepared for colder conditions than expected, even in summer, and always check the latest weather forecast before heading out. Winter conditions can include ice and heavy snow, making the trail very dangerous.

faqs.question: Are there any circular route options or alternatives to this direct ascent?
faqs.answer: While this specific route focuses on the direct ascent, there are indeed circular route possibilities in the Moncayo area. For instance, some riders choose to descend via the Collado de Castilla (Castilla Pass) to create a loop. Exploring these options might offer different perspectives and challenges.

waypoints.name: GR 90.1: Path from Ágreda to Ambel
